result:
The first thing I notice is the hum. Not the mechanical drone of the refrigerator, not the distant highway lullaby, but a subtler vibration, the sound of my own blood convincing me I’m still tethered to the planet. I press my ear to the pillow and the hum becomes an orchestra: cardiac drums, pulmonary strings, the high-frequency hiss of thought leaking through cracks I never agreed to install.  

Outside, the city is rehearsing its usual denial of darkness. Streetlights bloom like artificial dandelions, casting one-story shadows that pretend to be people. I used to think night was a single color, the same indigo from pole to pole, but now I see it is a collage of omitted light: neon that never quite touches the sky, headlights sweeping across walls like detectives searching for evidence of yesterday.  

Inside, the apartment feels both too large and too small, an accordion of memory and forgetting. The kitchen clock is seven minutes fast; I’ve never corrected it because the Future deserves a head start. On the windowsill the rosemary cutting I stole from a restaurant sidewalk is dying with suicidal slowness, its needles turning to parchment. I tell it, “Hold on, translate air into scent,” but chlorophyll is an honest employee who clocks out when the contract ends.  

Tonight I’m haunted by the idea that every object is a draft of itself. The chair is only temporarily a chair; tomorrow it might be kindling, tomorrow-next a museum label reading “early-century sitting-device, presumed functional.” I am likewise revision. My reflection keeps pushing updated software to my face, patching bugs called youth, optimism, the ability to read 10-point font at arm’s length. I accept the patches; I have no admin privileges.  

I open the junk drawer—unofficial archive of the obsolete—and discover a ticket stub for a concert I don’t remember attending. The band’s name is half worn away, a palimpsest of ink and sweat. Did I dance? Did I kiss anyone during the slow song? The paper is too polite to remind me. I consider the possibility that I’ve been assigned somebody else’s nostalgia, that memories are traded like baseball cards in some shadow bazaar. If so, I want to file a complaint: the album attached to this ticket is terrible, all angst and sequenced heartbeats.  

I move to the balcony because walls feel editorial. The air has the temperature of unwashed sheets, thick with the rumor of rain. Across the alley, a neighbor I’ve never met practices silent saxophone: fingers moving over valves, cheeks billowing, but no sound reaches me. I watch anyway, a consumer of mute jazz, and imagine the solo as a rope thrown between our separate ledges. If I grabbed it, would I be pulled into his narrative or yanked out of mine?  

Below, a delivery cyclist flicks through stop-sign sermons, insulated by the righteousness of hunger. His insulated bag glows like treasure. I picture the food inside—Thai basil sliding off a wok, cheese surrendering to gravity, steam drafting resignation letters to the sky. Somewhere a customer refreshes a tracking app, a tiny god loitering over a dot that represents dinner. The gap between dot and meal is a canyon we call patience.  

I go back in, carrying the city on my clothes. The humming is louder now, perhaps because I’ve named it, perhaps because naming is the first step toward escalation. I sit at the table with no purpose and realize the laptop is open, cued to a blank document titled “Untitled 73.” The cursor blinks like a lighthouse suffering an existential crisis: keep ships safe or lull them toward doom? I touch a key; the cursor flinches, as if my fingerprint burns.  

I type: “The first thing I notice is the hum.” Then I stop. The sentence feels both original and shoplifted. I wonder who else is writing this exact line tonight, how many first things hum across how many time zones. Maybe we are nodes in a planetary hive, producing similar confessions so the universe can compare notes on what it feels like to be half matter, half longing.  

I delete the line. The cursor returns to zero—an electronic ouroboros tasting its own absence. I think about the rosemary, the concert stub, the silent saxophone. Ephemera petitioning for tenure. I decide the only honest document would be a single blank page emailed to everyone I’ve disappointed, subject line: “Exhibit A, your honor.” But attachments exceed memory limits; regret is high-resolution.  

Instead I open a new tab and search “how to save a dying herb indoors.” The answers are communally tender: more sun, less water, whispered compliments. I whisper to the rosemary, “You are the smell of Sunday lamb, the finger rubbed behind a lover’s ear, the proof that leaves agree to be useful.” The plant does not respond, but plants are introverts; they answer by persisting or by not.  

I close the laptop. The humming softens, as if it approves of disconnection. I notice my own breathing again, how it matches the rhythm of traffic lights changing three stories down: inhale green, exhale red. Somewhere between lungs and LEDs, I feel the small mercy of alignment. It is not meaning—meaning is a luxury item—but it is cadence, and cadence can carry you across nights that refuse to disclose their exit wounds.  

I decide to leave the clock fast, water the rosemary once more, tuck the concert stub into the pages of a book I haven’t finished. That way, years from now, a future self (draft 12 or 20) will encounter the paper, pause, and perhaps feel the tug of a rope thrown from a balcony across time. Whether he pulls me in or I pull him out is irrelevant; the transaction itself will be the song, finally audible, with or without saxophone.  

I turn off the lamp. In the manufactured darkness the apartment expands, each object reclaiming its unused negative space. I lie down. The hum resumes its vigil, and I realize it was never mine alone; it is a rental, communal as moonlight. I close my eyes, not to sleep but to sign the lease again, nightly ritual of tenancy in the body. Outside, the city keeps deleting stars, but inside the orchestra plays on—violins of vein, percussion of skull—enough music, almost, to believe the chair will stay a chair until morning.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
